Member-only story

Most JavaScript developers don’t misunderstand hoisting — they misunderstand how JavaScript builds and executes programs.

9 min read
Just now

--

There are a few JavaScript topics that seem impossible to escape.
Closures.
The Event Loop.
this.
And, of course, hoisting.
Every few months, someone posts another explanation with colorful diagrams showing variables floating to the top of a file. Someone else insists that “nothing is actually moved.” Another developer argues about Temporal Dead Zones, function declarations, or execution contexts.
The debate never ends.
What’s interesting is that none of those explanations address the real problem.
After years of writing JavaScript — and reviewing code written by developers with varying levels of experience — I’ve noticed something surprising:
Most production bugs that people blame on hoisting have almost nothing to do with hoisting.
They come from a flawed mental model of how JavaScript executes code.
Hoisting is simply the first place where that mental model breaks.
